coredata.dc:title: Molecular investigation on interfacial behaviors between carbon dots and dopamine: The highly pH-dependent self-assembly dynamics of nanocarriers
coredata.prism:publicationName: Colloids and Surfaces A: Physicochemical and Engineering Aspects

coredata.dc:description: This work devotes to provide insights into the synthesis of nanosystems based on carbon dots (CDs) and dopamine (DPA) by molecular dynamics (MD) simulations, helping to guide the development of carbon-based nanocarriers for advanced cancer therapies. The solution pH is proved to be crucial for the self-assembly of CDs-DPA, and the hydrogen bonding (H-bonding) between the carboxyl groups on CDs and the polar groups in DPA is the driving force for the interfacial interaction. The affinity of CDs to DPA is strengthened with the pH rising under acidic conditions, due to the increasement of ionized carboxyl groups on CDs, whereas the trend reverses under alkaline conditions as the dominant DPA species transforms from cationic to anionic. Desirable self-assembly performances of CDs-DPA can be achieved under different conditions. CDs obtain the greatest compaction of DPA coating in the acidic condition of pH< 6.5, but the highest adsorption strength to DPA in weakly alkaline condition due to the most active H-bonding interaction. At pH∼10, DPA is more inclined to aggregate and adsorb on CDs in the form of clusters. Sodium ions are found to be capable of mediating anionic DPA adsorbed onto CDs by means of electrostatic attraction under strongly alkaline conditions.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Molecular structure of the CD (a) and its functionalization (b).

Protonate/deprotonate equilibria for DPA. DH2NH3 + represents the positively charged cationic DPA species with a protonated primary amine, DH−NH3 + represents the zwitterionic DPA species with a protonated primary amine and a deprotonated phenolic hydroxyl, DH2NH2 represents the nonionic neutral DPA species, DH−NH2 represents the negatively charged anionic DPA species with one deprotonated phenolic hydroxyl, and D2−NH2 represents the negatively charged anionic DPA species with two deprotonated phenolic hydroxyls [23,32,33].
Transition of protonated/deprotonated states of DPA as a function of solution pH value.
Initial conformation of a CD-DPA system, in which the cyan licorice-like models represent DPA molecules, and the lime transparent body represents the water box.
Illustration of Steered MD and the generation of configurations for umbrella sampling.

RDFs of the amines (a) and phenolic hydroxyls (b) of DPA and water molecules (c) in Model I∼IV, where NA represents the nitrogen (N) atom in amine, OP represents the oxygen (O) atom in phenolic hydroxyl, and OW and HW represent the O and H atoms of water molecules, respectively.
Four different patterns of DPA adsorbed on CDs surface, which are induced by amine (a), phenolic hydroxyl (b), amine-phenolic hydroxyl synergy (c) and nonpolar groups (d), respectively.
RDFs of the amine (a) and phenolic hydroxyl (b) groups in Model V∼VIII.

Analysis of the pH-dependent CDs-DPA interfacial behaviors, including the statistics and classification of the DPA molecules adsorbed on CDs (a), the solvent accessible surface area of CDs and the contact area between CDs and DPA (b).
Statistics of the H-bonding interaction for Model I∼VIII.
Conformations of CDs-DPA self-assembly in Model I∼VIII. The animations of CDs-DPA self-assembly are shown in Video SV1.
PMF profiles and typical adsorption states of DH2NH3 + in Model i∼iii (a∼c).
PMF profiles of different DPA species.
Number evolution of HBOH and HBNH (a∼d) and frequency distribution of their distance (e∼h) /angle (i∼l) for DH2NH3 +, DH−NH3 +, DH2NH2 and DH−NH2.
Displacement distribution and cloud map of time-space density distribution of −NH2, −O−, −OH and Na+ in Model vi (a) and vii (b).
Parameter setting for the molecular models of CDs.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This work devotes to provide insights into the synthesis of nanosystems based on carbon dots (CDs) and dopamine (DPA) by molecular dynamics (MD) simulations, helping to guide the development of carbon-based nanocarriers for advanced cancer therapies. The solution pH is proved to be crucial for the self-assembly of CDs-DPA, and the hydrogen bonding (H-bonding) between the carboxyl groups on CDs and the polar groups in DPA is the driving force for the interfacial interaction. The affinity of CDs to DPA is strengthened with the pH rising under acidic conditions, due to the increasement of ionized carboxyl groups on CDs, whereas the trend reverses under alkaline conditions as the dominant DPA species transforms from cationic to anionic. Desirable self-assembly performances of CDs-DPA can be achieved under different conditions. CDs obtain the greatest compaction of DPA coating in the acidic condition of pH< 6.5, but the highest adsorption strength to DPA in weakly alkaline condition due to the most active H-bonding interaction. At pH∼10, DPA is more inclined to aggregate and adsorb on CDs in the form of clusters. Sodium ions are found to be capable of mediating anionic DPA adsorbed onto CDs by means of electrostatic attraction under strongly alkaline conditions.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Benefitting from the progress in the application of nanomaterials, nanocarriers (NCs) have become one of the promising approaches utilized in advanced cancer treatment, aimed to overcome the harmful side effects of traditional therapies, like surgery, radiotherapy and chemotherapy, etc. [1,2] The NCs for medicinal purposes are designed to load and deliver chemotherapeutic drugs, photosensitizers and imaging agents, etc., in order to achieve synergistic therapies combined targeted therapy and photothermal/photodynamic therapy (PTT/PDT), etc. [2,3] Carbon dots (CDs) are one of the most versatile carbon-based nanomaterials commonly applied in drug delivery and cancer treatment due to its unique physicochemical properties [4]. CDs are quasi-spherical particles consisting of multilayer graphene with dimensions below 10 nm [5,6]. A high density of carboxylic acid moieties exposed on the CDs surface endows them with excellent water solubility and abundant binding sites for therapeutic agent loading or subsequent functionalization with various organic, polymeric, inorganic, or biological species [6–8]. PTT/PDT and imaging of tumor tissue can also be realized via CDs-based NCs due to its significant photo-triggered properties [9]. However, untreated CDs may be incapable of yielding ideal surface charge state, geometrical characteristic or fluorescence properties, resulting undesirable in vitro drug-loading capacity or in vivo deliverability/release rate and even toxicity to the normal tissue [2,10]. Therefore, the CDs-based NCs usually need to have their surfaces fully passivated to ensure medical efficacy.

The mussel-inspired material, polydopamine (PDA), has been a great choice for surface passivation or functional extension of CDs-based NCs [11,12]. It features great biocompatibility, helping to shield the cytotoxicity of nanoparticles[13]. Pappalardo et al. [14] investigated the application of CDs as imaging probes in in vitro/vivo model systems, confirming the reduced cell toxicity of the PDA-passivated CDs in comparison to their counterpart without modification by PDA. The one-pot route of using PDA as the carbon sources of CDs is usually aimed to obtain its photo-triggered properties [15,16]. Li et al. [15] synthesized a multifunctional CDs-PDA NCs for the imaging-guided photothermal therapy targeted to liver tumor cells, where the epithelial cell adhesion molecule antibody and tetravalent platinum prodrug were also attached onto CDs-PDA by the amidate reaction. The NCs displayed stable photothermal property and considerable anti-tumor therapeutic effect in vivo. However, PDA is best known for its excellent adhesion and sensitive responsiveness. It is also often introduced into NCs through its in-situ polymerization and graft onto the surface of CDs, thus helping to achieve the controllable drug loading/release, manifested as its molecular-level dynamic behavior in response to various stimuli (e.g., pH, temperature or irradiation). [17–20]. Sun et al. [17] prepared supramolecular hybrids of CDs and doxorubicin (DOX), however, a rapid separation of DOX from CDs occurred in fetal bovine serum solution due to the poor stability, whereas the hybrids with PDA coating displayed enhanced stability in cell culture media, producing a slow release of DOX in living cells. As stated above, the introduction of PDA has been proved to be capable of expanding the functionality of CDs-based NCs in therapies.

Generally, PDA generates from dopamine (DPA), whose polymerization is quite time-consuming (usually takes hours) [21,22], therefore, the adsorption of DPA on CDs surface should be the first step in the synthesis of CDs-based NCs. As proposed in the work of Chai et al. [20], the primary amine of DPA can be catalyzed by 1-ethyl-3-(3-dimethyl aminopropyl) carbodiimide/N-hydroxy-succinimide (i.e., EDC/NHS) to form an amide with the carboxyl groups on CDs surface. An active CDs-DPA interfacial interaction is favorable for achieving chemical connections between them, which depends on the adsorption affinity of CDs to DPA. However, the ionization equilibrium process of DPA is highly pH dependent, attributed to its catechol-amine functional groups with high pH-sensitivity [23]. CDs will also be characterized with different ratios of ionized acidic oxygenic groups (mainly carboxyl groups) as pH changing [8]. These make the aqueous solution with the coexistence of CDs and DPA has a complicated ionic situation varied with pH, thereby involving intricated physical transitions into the self-assembly of CDs-PDA, including protonation/deprotonation of polar groups, intermolecular /intramolecular hydrogen bonding (H-bonding) interactions, electrostatic attraction/repulsion and molecular topology changes, etc.[24,25] Unspecified pH conditions will result in unpredictable diffusive/agglomerate behavior of DPA at the very initial stages of CDs-DPA NCs synthesis. Therefore, the underlying mechanism of pH-dependent intermolecular interactions between CDs and DPA needs to be elucidated at molecular scale, in order to determine the optimal pH conditions of CDs-PDA hybridization, which will be helpful to achieve the scheme optimization for the synthesis of organic-inorganic hybrid NCs.

Molecular modeling and simulation methods have already been an integral part for the investigation of the microscopic details of “Intermolecular Interfacial Interaction”, competent in analyzing the dynamical processes of self-assembly of various polymers and drug molecules on carbon-based NPs [26–29]. Therefore, molecular dynamics (MD) simulations were adopted as the main approach in this work, with the focus on the initial stage of supramolecular self-assembly in physiological solution environment, attempting to investigate the adsorption dynamics of DPA on the CDs surface and its pH-dependency. The molecular models of CDs and DPA were built with different states of protonation/ deprotonation, referred to their pK a values in the ionization of each order. The interfacial interaction of CDs-DPA complex under different pH conditions were comprehensively analyzed, taking the aggregating state, noncovalent interactions, adsorption energy, etc. as the characteristic items.

To determine the molecular models of CDs and DPA is crucial for the reasonable reflection of the pH conditions in solution. The parameterization of the CDs models in this study was basically referred to the work of Alves et al. [8], in which CDs were quantified to be covered with varieties of oxygenic groups with a density of ∼5 mmol/g, and almost 65% of the oxygenic groups are acidic carboxyl groups with pK a < 6. The initial structures of CDs were generated by using the Carbon Dot Builder (http://cd-builder.upol.cz), a plug-in built upon the graphene and nanotube builder in VMD [30]. As shown in Fig. 1a, the CDs are quasi-spherical nanoparticles composed of 11 layers of hexagonal graphene nanosheet, in the manner of non-covalent arrangement with a interlayer spacing of 3.35 Å. Referring to the researches conducted by Alves and Paloncýová et al. [27], three different types of oxygenic groups, i.e., carboxyl, phenolic hydroxyl and carbonyl (neutral and deprotonated) were identified as the major oxygenic groups, which were randomly grafted onto CDs (Fig. 1b) [8,27]. The unfunctionalized carbon atoms distributed on the surface of CDs were capped with hydrogen (H) atoms.

Based on the quantitative study for the abundances of each type of oxygenic group [8], the percentages of carboxyl, phenolic hydroxyl and carbonyl groups were set to 64%, 18% and 18%, respectively, which are rounded-off values according to the actual CDs models established in this work ( Table 1). The ratio of deprotonated and neutral carboxyl groups on CDs (denoted as −COO− CDs and −COOHCDs, respectively) for simulation systems at different values of pH can be calculated by Henderson-Hasselbalch equation [31]:

- and N HA represent the number of deprotonated and neutral carboxyl groups, respectively. As estimated by Alves et al., the pK a of −COOHCDs is 3–6 [8], hence the maximum pH value of the solution can be determined by plugging the upper limit of pK
a into Eq. 1. Based on the above discussion, a series of CDs models were constructed with different ratios of −COO− CDs, corresponding to the approximate range of pH values in different solution environments, which have been illustrated in Table 1 with more detailed information. Detailed information about the construction of CDs model for MD simulation are given in Supplement Information.

Obviously, unless a strongly acidic solution environment is present, the surface of CDs will be negatively charged resulting from the large coverage of −COO− CDs. For pH above 7.4, the quantity of −COO− CDs will be at least 20–30 times larger than that of −COOHCDs. As the −COO− CDs absolutely dominate on the surfaces of CDs under strongly alkaline conditions, its surface coverage can be approximated as 100% in simulations. As stated above, the charge strength of CDs surface will be highly distinguished within an extremely small variation range of solution pH values, which will inevitably affect the adsorption dynamics of DPA on CDs.

Similar to CDs, a DPA molecule also can transform to a series of protonate/deprotonate states, due to its three different ionizable sites, including a primary amine and two phenolic hydroxyls, among which, the phenolic hydroxyl group in the ortho position of the aminoethyl is easier to be ionized than the other one [23]. Fig. 2 shows the protonate/deprotonate equilibria of DPA, which leads to the five different charged/uncharged species. The equilibrium constants for different ionization reactions are given in Fig. 2 [23,32,33].

According to the pK a of DPA at the corresponding protonated/deprotonated state (Fig. 2), the fraction of each type of DPA species is calculated as a function of pH ( Fig. 3) on the basis of Eq. 1, which exhibits the protonated/deprotonated state of DPA shifting with the pH of solution. As shown in Fig. 3, the fraction of DH2NH2 keeps low (<5.2%) over the entire range of the available pH values, whereas the cationic species of DH2NH3 + dominate in the acidic solution environment (pH<7). The zwitterionic species DH−NH3 + will surge under conditions of a weakly alkaline solution, but then will give way to anionic species (i.e., DH−NH2 and D2−NH2) as the pH rises further. It can be inferred that the aqueous solvated DPA is mostly ionic and should not be treated as non-ionized electroneutral species in the MD simulations.

In order to investigate the self-assembly dynamic of CDs-DPA molecular system, a set of MD models (Model I∼VIII, Table 2) were first established, each of which has a CD molecule model placed in the center of the water box with a dimension of 9.5 × 9.5 × 9.5 nm3, surrounded by 350 DPA molecules that were randomly distributed in the box ( Fig. 4). To reflect the pH states of different solution environment, the ratio of −COO− CDs groups on the surface of CD, and the percentage of each types of DPA species in Model I∼VIII are arranged as shown in Table 2. It should be noted that, in Model I∼IV (pH < 6.5), the ratio of −COO− CDs increases from 0 to 75% as the pH increases, with only the cationic species (i.e., DH2NH3 +) presented, whereas the DPA species in model V∼VIII (pH > 7.4) are diversified into five different types, with the ratio of −COO− CDs on each CD remained 100%. The TIP3P model [34] was chosen to represent the water molecules, and sodium ion (Na+) and chloride ions (Cl−) were added with a density of 0.154 mol/L (same as normal saline) after neutralizing the charges from −COO− CDs and protonated/deprotonated DPA species.

Classical MD simulations were carried out by GROMACS code [35]. All-atom optimized potential for liquid simulations force field (OPLS-AA) was employed [36], in which the intra-/intermolecular non-bonded interactions were described by the Lennard-Jones 12–6 (LJ12–6) parameters. The LJ12–6 and electrostatic interaction parameters for CDs were referred to the work of Paloncýová et al. [27] (Table S1), where the partial charges of atoms in CDs were derived from the DFT-based calculation. The intermolecular interactions between the different layers of CDs were not subject to any extra constraints except LJ12–6 or electrostatic potential. The bonded and non-bonded interaction parameters for the DPA species were obtained from the web server LigParGen [37], which is capable of providing reliable OPLS-AA parameters for organic molecules with less than 200 atoms. DFT-based calculations were introduced in this work by the quantum chemistry calculation program ORCA [38] to characterize the electronic structure of DPA species. The geometric optimization and frequency analysis were performed at B3LYP functional level [39] with def2-TZVP basis set [40] using Solvation Model Density models [41], and the singlet point energies were calculated on the basis of the optimized non-imaginary frequency structures at M06–2X functional level [42] with 6–311 +G* basis set [43–45]. 1.2 *CM5 charges were chosen to describe the partial charges of DPA species as recommended by the developers of OPLS force field [46], which were calculated by the wave function analysis program Multiwfn [47]. Detailed information about the definition of force field parameters are given in Supplement Information (Table S1).

Each simulation system, including Model I∼VIII, went through the stages of energy minimization and pre-equilibrium (in both canonical and isobaric-isothermal ensembles for 1 ns) before production run. The translation or rotation of the centroid of DPA and CDs were temporarily fixed at the pre-equilibrium stage, during which only the water molecules were allowed to move freely, in order to reach a reasonable initial distribution at the specified temperature (T = 310 K) and pressure (P = 1Bar). Therefore, a robust solution environment was created for the simulation of CDs-DPA self-assembly. The DPA and CDs were unfixed at the stage of production run, and the DPA molecules were allowed to diffuse freely in isobaric-isothermal ensemble. The production runs of Model I∼VIII lasted for 40 ns at a time step of 1 fs, with the temperature and pressure maintained at T = 310 K (using v-rescal algorithm) and P = 1Bar (Parrinello-Rahman barostat), respectively. Detailed computational settings of MD simulations were provided in Table S2 in Supplement Information. The visual analysis of simulation results was carried out by program Visual Molecular Dynamics (VMD) [30].

In addition, the free energies of DPA adsorbed on the surface of CDs were estimated by the potentials of mean force (PMF) calculations using umbrella sampling. Therefore, another seven MD models, Model i∼vii ( Table 3), were built for the PMF calculation. In this section, we focused on the analysis of adsorption strength of individual DPA molecules, attempting to discover the impact of −COO− CDs ratio on the adsorption energies of DPA and the adsorption patterns of five different DPA solvated species on the surface of CDs. For this purpose, the CDs models with varied ratios of −COO− CDs were adopted in Model i∼iii, where only DH2NH3 + were taken, since DH2NH3 + is the exclusive species of DPA that can coexist with unionized −COOHCDs in solutions. On the contrary, the ratios of −COO− CDs in Model i and Model iv∼vii were 100% without exception, but the species of DPA were different. As shown in Fig. 5, Steered Molecular Dynamics (SMD) simulations were carried out to generate the instantaneous configurations of DPA detaching from CDs, which started with a single DPA molecule got pulled away from the same site of CDs surface. The configurations were taken along the reaction coordinate for each SMD case with a minimum number of ten, and the space between two adjacent windows ranged from 0.10 to 0.15 nm. The harmonic potential with spring constant of k = 1000–8000 kJ/mol was exerted on the centroid of mass (COM) of DPA to conduct the umbrella sampling. Each configuration was sampled for 5 ns after a short equilibration process of 0.5 ns. The distance between the COM of CD and the COM of DPA molecule was employed as reference distance. The PMF profiles were extracted by the weighted histogram analysis method (WHAM) [48].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effects of polar groups on pH-dependent self-assembly of CDs-DPA
ce:section.ce:para: The existence of polar groups is usually the inducement for adsorption behavior, therefore, the amines and phenolic hydroxyls in DPA and the acidic oxygenic groups on CDs should take responsibility for the non-covalent interactions at the interface of CDs and DPA. Surface-based radial distribution function (RDF) was introduced in this work to investigate the spatial distribution of the amine and phenolic hydroxyl groups of DPA to the surface of CDs. Different from the methodology of traditional RDF, the calculation of surface-based RDF takes the shortest distance between the objective atom set and the reference atom set into account, and gives the unnormalized profile with more pronounced peaks to highlight the distribution pattern of objective atom sets. The RDFs of the amine and phenolic hydroxyl groups in Model I∼VIII were averaged from the molecular motion of the final 10 ns of production run. Fig. 6 shows the RDF profiles of Model I∼IV inset with the typical conformations of adsorbed molecules, in order to reflect the effect of the ratios of −COO− CDs on the spatial distribution of DPA and water molecules under the acidic conditions (pH < 6.5).

For Model I, the RDF profile of amines does not show any obvious peaks (Fig. 6a), while the amplitude of RDF profile of phenolic hydroxyls also remains the lowest among all the available models (Fig. 6b), indicating that it is hard for DPA to adsorb onto CDs under strongly acidic conditions (pH<4) with the absence of −COO− CDs. As the pH rises, manifested as the incremental −COO− CDs moieties of Model II∼IV, the affinity of CDs to DPA molecules is promoted, which are reflected by the increasing heights of RDF peaks of Model II∼IV (Fig. 6). This phenomenon should be ascribed to the preference of cationic DPA (i.e., DH2NH3 +) for −COO− CDs rather than −COOHCDs.

Combining the above RDF-based discussion and the conformations of adsorbed DPA/water molecules shown in the insets of Fig. 6, it can be speculated that the H-bonding must be the driving force for CDs to attract DPA and water molecules, where the O atoms of −COO− CDs or −COOHCDs usually act as the receptors of H-bonds. In Fig. 6, the amines and phenolic hydroxyls in Model II∼IV all present three significant peaks in RDF profiles. The first peaks stand for the H atoms in the polar groups, located at a distance of 1.6 Å from the H-bond receptors provided by −COO− CDs /−COOHCDs; the second peaks stand for the N atoms in amines (NA) or the O atoms in phenolic hydroxyls (OP) acting as the H-bond donors, which are located at distances of 2.5 Å and 2.6 Å, respectively, from the H-bond receptors; and the third peaks can be attributed to the rest of the atoms in the polar groups of DPA which are not involved in H-bonding interactions.

The RDFs of water molecules shown in Fig. 6c implies the coverage of water layers on CDs, with the first two peaks (1.8 Å and 2.7 Å) representing the H and O atoms in water molecules, respectively. In the cases with low ratio of −COO− CDs (Model I and II), higher proportions of water molecules provide their O atoms as receptors to form H-bonds with the H atoms in −COOHCDs. As the ratio of −COO− CDs rises with the increase of pH, the interactions between DPA and CDs seem more active, and a larger number of water molecules covering on CDs will be replaced by DPA, which explains the decreasing trend of the amplitude of RDF profile for the water molecules in Model I∼IV (Fig. 6c).

To further characterize the adsorption behavior of DPA on CDs, a vector denoted as the yellow 3D arrow in the inset of Fig. 6a∼b was defined for each DPA molecule to describe its orientation toward the surface of CDs, which takes the NA atoms in the amine of DPA as the starting point (the ball head) and the centroids of the OP atoms in the two phenolic hydroxyls of DPA as the ending point (the arrow head). Four types of adsorption postures of DPA are figured out as shown in Fig. 7, corresponding to four different patterns of adsorption, which are induced by (a) amine, (b) phenolic hydroxyl, (c) amine-phenolic hydroxyl synergy and (d) nonpolar groups in sequence.

As shown in Fig. 7a and b, the adsorption induced only by the amine or phenolic hydroxyl group are reflected as the predefined vectors with their arrows or ball heads pointing to the CDs surface, whereas, the predefined vectors for the adsorption induced by the synergy of amine and phenolic hydroxyl (Fig. 7c) seem approximately tangent to the CDs surface. In addition, there will be a small amount of DPA adsorbed on the top or bottom of CDs as shown in Fig. 7d, where the sp2 graphene can form non-covalent interaction with the non-polar functional groups (i.e., benzene rings or ethyl groups) in DPA. The classification for the postures and patterns of DPA molecules adsorbed on CDs will be helpful to understand the interaction mechanism of CDs-DPA assembly under different pH conditions.

Fig. 8 shows the RDF of the amine and phenolic hydroxyls of DPA in Model V∼VIII, where the ratio of −COO− CDs are 100% without exception, but the deprotonation states of DPA are different from each other, indicating that they are under different alkaline conditions. In Model V (pH∼7.4) and Model VI (pH∼8.5), DPA shows the greatest affinity to CDs, inferring from the higher RDF peaks than those in Model VII∼VIII (Fig. 8). Since the cation DH2NH3 + is the dominant species of DPA in Model V and VI, it can respond positively to the electrostatic attraction of the negatively charged CDs surface. In Model VII, the major DPA species is the zwitterion DH−NH3 + characterized by one ionized phenol hydroxyl, which will prevent the DPA from adsorbed on CDs resulting from the electrostatic repulsion of the negatively charged CDs surface, and may also be adverse to the amine-induced adsorption. This can be seen from the much lower RDF peaks of DPA in Model VII.

Under the strongly alkaline condition of Model VIII (pH∼12), the DPA molecules are dominated by the anionic species (DH−NH2 and D2−NH2). Similar to the strongly acidic situation (Model I, Fig. 6a), no obvious RDF peak of the amines in Model VIII is shown either (Fig. 8a), which indicates that the contribution of amines to the adsorption of DPA on CDs is limited under the strongly acidic or alkaline conditions. However, the RDF profile of phenolic hydroxyls for the DPA in Model VIII display a sharp peak centering at r = 3.1 Å (Fig. 8b), which is much further away from the CDs surface than those in Model I∼VI (1.6 Å and 2.6 Å, Fig. 6b and Fig. 8b). These suggest an intermolecular adsorption pattern distinguishing from the H-bonding between CDs and unionized phenol hydroxyls in DPA, which is attributed to the intermediation of Na+ in the solution as shown in the inset of Fig. 8b. More detailed discussion about this Na+-involved phenomenon will be conducted in Section 3.4.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Impacts of pH on CDs-DPA interfacial behaviors
ce:section.ce:para: As shown in Fig. 6 and Fig. 8, the RDF peaks of amines, phenolic hydroxyls and water molecules were ended at 3.4–3.5 Å. Therefore, in this work, 3.5 Å was defined as the distance cutoff to determine the contacting relationship of DPA and water molecules with CDs. Specifically, once a DPA molecule with at least one atom reaching to the neighborhood within 3.5 Å of the CDs, this molecule will be considered as adsorbed on CDs. This criterion was adopted in this section to quantify the pH-dependent interfacial affinities of CDs, DPA and water molecules.

Fig. 9a shows the number of DPA molecules captured by CDs in Model I∼VIII, which was counted on the basis of the final 10 ns of production run for each model, and have been subdivided into four different types according to the adsorption patterns illustrated in Fig. 7. Overall, as the value of pH rises, the amount of adsorbed DPA molecules increases first and then decreases (Fig. 9a). The uptrend should be ascribed to the incremental of −COO− CDs ratios in the acidic solutions from Model I to Model V, whereas the downtrend results from the transition of DPA species in the alkaline solutions of Model VI∼VIII. Under strongly acidic or alkaline conditions (e.g., Model I, II and VIII), the phenolic hydroxyls interact with CDs more actively than amines, but the total amount of adsorbed DPA remains at the lowest level. By contrast, a weakly acidic or alkaline solution (Model III∼VI) is more favorable for the adsorption of DPA on CDs, where the amines are more advantageous in inducing adsorption. As the solution transforms from acidic to alkaline, the leading group for adsorption shifts from phenolic hydroxyl to amine. Especially in Model V, where both the ratio of −COO− CDs and the fraction of cation DH2NH3 + are close to 100%, corresponding to a weakly alkaline condition with pH∼7.4, the adsorption capacity of CDs to DPA reached the maximum.

As shown in the insets of the Fig. 9b, the surface of CDs was entirely covered by solvent at the pre-equilibrium stage where DPA have not yet interacted with CDs. In the stage of production run when DPA is adsorbed onto CDs, parts of solvent molecules on CDs will be replaced by DPA molecules, and DPA will occupy the surface area of CDs to a certain extent, thereby reducing the solvent accessible surface area (SASA) of CDs. To assess the stability of the DPA coating on CDs surface, the contact area of CDs and DPA were calculated from the data of the final 10 ns production run (Fig. 9b), which (Contact Area CDs-DPA) were obtained by Eq. 2:
(2)
Contact
Area
CDc - DPA
=
SASA
CDs
−
SASA
CDs - DPA
where the SASACDs and SASACDs-DPA represent the SASA of CDs excluding and including the DPA molecules adsorbed on their surface, respectively, as illustrated in the insets of Fig. 9b. As the green dashed curve of Fig. 9b shows, the SASACDs (identified with the surface area of CDs) in Model I∼VIII are about 80 nm2, which is a reasonable value for CDs with diameters of 3.7 to 4.0 nm. It should be noted that Model I and II present a slightly larger value of SASACDs than that of others, due to their higher proportion of −COOHCDs with more solvent-accessible unionized protons. Beyond that, SASACDs did not present any significant pH-relevance.

By contrast, the curve of Contact Area CDs-DPA shows a highly pH-dependent trend opposite to that of SASACDs-DPA (Fig. 9b), which also accords well with the trend of the number of DPA adsorbed on CDs (Fig. 9a). However, it is unordinary that the value of Contact Area CDs-DPA for Model IV is the greatest, yet it does not have the greatest amount of adsorbed DPA on CDs, and the CD in Model V achieves almost the same Contact Area CDs-DPA as that in Model III, when the former adsorbs the most DPA molecules than all the other models (Fig. 9a). By further analyzing the count of DPA molecules in different adsorption patterns, it can be found that the number of adsorbed DPA molecules induced by phenolic hydroxyls is larger in Model IV than that in Model V, when the case for amine inducement is quite the reverse. Therefore, it is reasonable to assume that phenolic hydroxyl is more competitive than amine in preempting the adsorption site from the pre-occupied water molecules on surface of CDs. This can explain that, although the CD in Model V achieves the most adsorbed DPA, it is the phenolic hydroxyls in DPA that can more effectively promote the desorption of pre-occupied water from CDs and thus enable the DPA molecules in Model III to reach the same level of Contact Area CDs-DPA as that in Model V.

The above analysis seems insufficient to explain why the CD-DPA system in Model VIII presents a large number of adsorbed DPA molecules induced by phenolic hydroxyls (red column, Fig. 9a) inconsistent with its smallest Contact Area CDs-DPA (blue line, Fig. 9b). However, referring to the RDF of the phenolic hydroxyls in Fig. 8b, it can be seen that the most pronounced peak of Model VIII appears at ∼3.1 Å, which implies an adsorption mechanism of Na+-mediated electrostatic attraction as the inset of Fig. 8b shows, rather than ∼2.6 Å and ∼1.6 Å standing for the H-bonding between phenolic hydroxyls and −COOHCDs /−COO− CDs. This indicates that the phenolic hydroxyl-induced adsorption of DPA in Model VIII is barely contributed by H-bonding but the intermediation of Na+, resulting from the dominant anionic species of DPA (i.e., DH−NH2) in Model VIII which is seemingly unable to directly H-bond with −COOHCDs /−COO− CDs. Therefore, to be precise, the phenol hydroxyl-induced H-bonding is mainly responsible for the desorption of pre-occupied water molecules during the self-assembly of CDs-DPA, whereas the amines-induced and Na+-mediated adsorption of DPA has less advantages to compete with water molecules for more Contact Area CDs-DPA.

Based on the above discussion, it can be found that H-bonding between CDs and DPA plays a crucial role in determining their interfacial behavior. Besides, as presented from Model IV to VI, both the species of DPA and the ratio of −COO− CDs change as the solution transitions from acidic to alkaline property. The effects of these two variables on the self-assembly of CDs-DPA need to be decoupled, which requires investigating the intermolecular H-bonding within the supramolecular system of DPA. Therefore, we estimated the H-bonds existing between DPA and CDs in Model I∼VIII, as well as those exclusively formed among DPA molecules. The R-α geometric criterion adopted by the MD simulation program GROMACS were used to identify the formation of H-bond [49], in which R represents the cutoff distance between the donor and the receptor, and α represents the cutoff angle of the H-Bonded system as shown in the inset of Fig. 10. In this work, R and α were set to be 3.5 Å and 30◦, respectively, as recommended by GROMACS [35,50]. For the convenience of description, the H-bond formed between CDs and DPA is denoted as HBCDs-DPA, and the H-bond formed among DPA molecules is denoted as HBDPA.

It can be seen from Fig. 10 that, the activity of CDs-DPA H-bonding increases first and then declines as the value of pH rises, entirely consistent with the trend for the number of adsorbed DPA molecules induced by amines (Fig. 9a). It indicates the advantage of amines in the formation of H-bonds between DPA and CDs. Under the acidic condition, there is hardly any HBDPA, implying a poor self-gathering performance of DPA molecules. As shown in Fig. 11a∼d, the DPA molecules that have not been anchored to CDs in Model I∼IV float around dispersedly. By contrast, under the weakly alkaline condition (pH∼7.4), Model V presents the most actively HBCDs-DPA (Figure R10), demonstrating that the adsorption strength of CD-DPA in Model V is the highest among all the available models. The number profile of HBDPA keeps an upward trend in Model V, and further increases to nearly catch up with the number of HBCDs-DPA in Model VI. As shown in Fig. 11f and g, a reduction of floating DPA can be clearly observed, which shows tendency to get attached by the DPA adsorbed on CDs or gather to form clusters. In Model VII (pH∼10), the number of HBCDs-DPA decreased sharply, whereas the number of HBDPA surged to a high level, overwhelmingly inhibiting the formation of HBCDs-DPA. It can be inferred that, in an alkaline environment with pH∼10, the DPA molecules prefer to gather together instead of adsorbed onto CDs. Fig. 11g also shows that most of CD surface is occupied by water molecules rather than DPA, whereas DPA adsorb onto the CDs surface in form of clusters. When the pH of solution reaches ∼12 (Model VIII, Fig. 10), HBCDS-DPA or HBDPA is barely formed, and similar to Model I (pH < 4), the DPA molecules show minimal tendency to gather or adsorb onto CDs (Fig. 11h).
Supplementary material related to this article can be found online at doi:10.1016/j.jhazmat.2021.126226.

The following is the Supplementary material related to this article Video S1.
Video S1
Video SV1: Animation to show the self-assembly of CDs-DPA in Model I∼VIII (MP4).
.

Based on the above analysis, it can be concluded that both weakly acidic and weakly alkaline are conducive to the self-assembly of DPA and CDs but in different mechanisms. In weakly acidic solution (pH<6.5, −COO− CDs: 75%), the compaction of DPA adsorbed on CDs is the highest, producing a dense DPA coating. In weakly alkaline solution with pH∼7.4, the capacity of CDs for DPA adsorption is the greatest and the CDs-DPA H-bonding interaction keeps most active, presenting the strongest adsorption strength. As pH reaches ∼8.5, the DPA-CDs interfacial interaction is slightly weakened, but the aggregation tendency of DPA was gradually significant. The existence of mediating zwitterionic DPA molecules anchored on CDs is favorable for more DPA to be captured. When pH is about 10, although the adsorption of DPA and CDs is insufficient, DPA show the best gathering performance, enabling them to adsorb on CDs in form of clusters. In summary, the competition between adsorption and self-gathering leads to different dynamics of CDs-DPA self-assembly. A higher ratio of −COO− CDs promotes the DPA to achieve adsorption on the surface of CDs, whereas the rising fraction of zwitterionic species DH−NH3 + will drive DPA molecules to be more inclined to self-gathering. That is, when the zwitterion DH−NH3 + is dominant in the system, the sufficiency of −COO− CDs will no longer play a primary role in determining the adsorption of DPA.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of surface charge of CDs on DPA adsorption
ce:section.ce:para: The above analysis based on Model I∼VIII reveals the pH-dependent self-assembly dynamics of CDs-DPA NCs, where the mechanism of CDs-DPA interfacial adsorption is only summarized statistically, leaving the details to be further studied. Therefore, Model i∼vii were built to focus on their specific behaviors and adsorption strength by excluding the interactions among DPA molecules. The effect of surface charge of CDs on DPA adsorption is investigated on the basis of Model i∼iii, where DH2NH3 + species of DPA are adopted to reflect the solution environments (pH ≤ 7.4) with the ratio of −COO− CDs set as 100%, 50% and 0, respectively, to present the decreasing pH.

Fig. 12 shows the PMF profiles of DH2NH3 + molecules along their desorption paths from the surface of CDs, which present the energy required by a DH2NH3 + molecule to reach a certain distance from the surface of CDs. In this work, the boundary of box is selected as the zero point for potential energy, where the interaction between CDs and DH2NH3 + can be negligible, given that the PMF profiles present approximately constant around the point near the boundary of box. To visualize the simulation results, the typical adsorption states of DH2NH3 + are extracted from Model i∼iii and numbered in the insets of Fig. 12, which correspond to the particular positions on PMF profiles (e.g., potential wells or steps). The H-bonding interaction of CDs and DH2NH3 + for each adsorption state has also been studied. For convenience and clarity, the H-bonds participated by amine and phenolic hydroxyl are denoted as HBNH and HBOH, respectively, while the adsorption energy is denoted as ΔE.

It can be seen from Fig. 12 that, as the ratio of −COO− CDs decreases, the maximum ΔE of Model i∼iii (i.e., ΔE max,i, ΔE max,ii and ΔE max,iii) also declines. The distance between the COMs of CDs and DH2NH3 + required for them to achieve H-bonding is getting shorter, indicating that the conditions for adsorption become more favorable as the negative charge distributed on the surface of CDs decreases. For DH2NH3 + and CD in Model i, there are a maximum of three HBNH and two HBOH formed in state 1(Fig. 12a), which happens when they get closest. However, only three HBNH are required for DH2NH3 + and CDs to achieve their ΔE max,i (state 3, Fig. 12a). More participation of HBOH in adsorption will increase the PMF of Model i, i.e., reduce the ΔE as shown in state 1 and 2 (Fig. 12a). It manifests that ΔE is not always positively correlated with the number of H-bonds between CDs and DH2NH3 +. In Model ii, DH2NH3 + and CDs reached their ΔE max,ii in adsorption state 3, where one HBNH and one HBOH are formed as shown in Fig. 12b. Although the number of H-bonds can increase as the distance between DH2NH3 + and CDs reduces (state 1 and 2 in Fig. 12b), the value of ΔE cannot be promoted according to the rising PMF curve. In Model iii, ΔE shows a positive correlation with the number of H-bonds between DH2NH3 + and CDs with ΔE max,iii presenting in adsorption state 1 (Fig. 12c), where only one HBNH but two HBOH are formed.

Based on the above analysis, it suggests that amine in Model i and ii plays a leading role in the adsorption of DH2NH3 + on CDs, and the formation of HBNH with −COO− CDs is a prerequisite to the subsequent formation of HBOH. On the contrary, due to the absence of −COO− CDs in Model iii, the amine shows a weak tendency to be electrostatically attracted by CDs, and a HBNH may transform into a HBOH on the same reaction coordinate, as shown in the adsorption states 2/2′ and 3/3′ (Fig. 12c). Meanwhile, DH2NH3 + at a longer distance from CDs will deviate from the presetting adsorption path and adsorb on the top of CDs by π-π stacking, as shown in state 4 (Fig. 12c). This is essentially because the neutral −COOHCDs does not have superiority in holding attraction for amine over phenolic hydroxyl. Therefore, as the ratio of −COO− CDs decreases, the number of HBNH formed between DH2NH3 + and CDs is reduced from three to one, while the number of HBOH remains unaffected (two). It indicates that amine is more sensitive to the surface charge of CDs, and the positive contribution of phenolic hydroxyl actually results from the repulsion of amine by electrically neutral surface of CD. Other potential non-covalent interaction within the CDs-DPA system, such as π-π interaction, has been found to have no advantage over H-bonding in promoting the adsorption of DPA, given that the benzene ring of DPA is not observed to be capable of intercalating into the layers of CDs, and the DPA molecule adsorbed onto CDs through the π-π interaction (adsorption state 4 of Model iii, Fig. 12c) is also inferior to those through the H-bonding in adsorption energy.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Adsorption affinity of different DPA species to CDs
ce:section.ce:para: In alkaline solutions with pH ≥ 7.4, the variation in the adsorption affinity of CDs to DPA with pH can be mainly attributed to the transition of DPA species. In this section, Model i and iv∼vii were built to investigate the adsorption affinity of five different types of DPA species to CDs on the basis of PMF analysis. The species of DH2NH3 +, DH−NH3 +, DH2NH2, DH−NH2 and D2−NH2 were adopted as the only variable in the above five models, respectively, with the ratio of −COO− CDs set as 100% without exception, in order to reflect the solution environments of pH ≥ 7.4 (Table 3). The animations of five different DPA species interacting with CDs in Model i and iv∼vii are shown in Video SV2.
Supplementary material related to this article can be found online at doi:10.1016/j.jhazmat.2021.126226.

The following is the Supplementary material related to this article Video S2.
Video S2
Video SV2: Animation to show the H-bonding interaction of CDs and different species of DPA in Model i and Model iv∼vii (MP4).
.

Fig. 13 shows the PMF profiles of five different DPA species along their desorption paths from the surface of CDs. Clearly, the DPA species with protonated amine (−NH3 +), i.e., DH2NH3 + and DH−NH3 +, present the ΔE far surpassing the values of other species. The ΔE of DH−NH3 + is just slightly lower than that of DH2NH3 + in Model i (the ratio of −COO− CDs is 100%), and even greater than that of DH2NH3 + in Model ii (the ratio of −COO− CDs is 50%, Fig. 12b), which indicates that DH−NH3 + actually has a strong affinity to CDs. However, due to its properties of zwitterions, DH−NH3 + prefers to gather with other ionized DPA molecules, therefore, in Model VI with DH−NH3 + as the major species, DPA tends to form clusters (Fig. 11f) instead of being adsorbed onto CDs. As a result of the absence of ionicity, the ΔE of neutral species, i.e., DH2NH2, is much lower than that of the zwitterionic DH−NH3 + which has a same net charge of 0.

For the anionic species of DPA, i.e., DH−NH2 and D2−NH2, the distance they required to reach ΔE max is greater than other DPA species, and the profiles of their PMF also seem odd. The ΔE max,vi approximates 0, indicating that DH−NH2 can hardly achieve adsorption, which ascribes the intermolecular electrostatic repulsion from −COO− CDs to the deprotonated phenolic hydroxyl (−O−) of DH−NH2. However, for D2−NH2 in the complete deprotonation state of DPA, which lacks the conditions to form H-bonds with CDs, the value of ΔE max,vii is larger than that for DH−NH2 and DH2NH2, seemingly incompatible with the principle of electrostatic repulsion. The peculiar PMF profile of D2−NH2 is due to the interaction mediated by Na+ as it has already mentioned in previous sections, which we can recognize as a chelation-like adsorption pattern different from the H-bonding responsible for the adsorption of the non-anionic DPA species. It suggests that the Na+ mediated adsorption is not energetically inferior, which results in a non-monotonical evolution of ΔE max for CDs-DPA with the increasing deprotonation of DPA molecules. To reveal the interaction mechanism of CDs-DPA in the different adsorption patterns, the H-bonding and Na+ mediated adsorption between different DPA species and CDs were statistically analyzed on the basis of SMD simulation.

The H-bonding interaction in the adsorption of CDs-DPA was illustrated in Fig. 14, where the number evolution of H-bonds and the frequency distribution of distance/angle are evaluated for Model i and iv∼vi. The statistic works were conducted on the basis of the results from the first windows of the umbrella sampling, where the motion of DPA molecules are restricted within the immediate vicinity of the CDs surface. The H-bonding interaction of Model vii is absent in Fig. 14, since there is no H-bond formed between D2−NH2 and CDs. It should be noted that the frequency analysis of H-bonding distance/angle derives from the 5000 samples of each model over a 5 ns production run, however, only 100 samples are selected to show at equal intervals, in order to achieve a better visual effect of the amount of H-bonds (Fig. 14a∼d).

As shown in Fig. 14a, the common situation in Model i is the formation of three HBNH and one HBOH between DH2NH3 + and CDs, corresponding to the adsorption state 2 in Fig. 12a, which may transform into state 3 with only three HBNH formed, or state 1 with one more HBOH formed (Fig. 12a). The frequency distribution of H-bonding distance/angle in Model i shows a high degree of regularity. The receptor-donor distances of HBNH and HBOH are concentrated at a length of 2.5 Å, and 2.6 Å, respectively, which correspond to the second peaks of the RDF profiles for amines and phenolic hydroxyl in Fig. 8, where the first peaks of RDF profiles correspond to the distance between receptor and H atom. This proves that H-bonding is the main contribution to the CDs-DPA interaction. Fig. 14b shows that there are commonly three HBNH participating in the adsorption of DH−NH3 +, whereas the formation of one HBOH is only detected in 5 out of 5000 samples, which is omitted due to the sampling interval. Fig. 14f and j indicate that the frequency distributions of distance/angle of HBNH are significantly regular, but the formation of HBOH is nonspontaneous, completely caused by the restriction of DPA movement under SMD.

Fig. 14c shows that the adsorption of DH2NH2 in Model v usually involves two HBOH, which may have a chance to increase to three with a H atom in phenolic hydroxyl meeting the geometric criterion to form H-bonds with two different −COO− CDs. Only a small amount of HBNH is sometimes detected in Model v, which is also not fully shown in Fig. 14c due to the sampling interval. In contrast to Model iv, the frequency distribution of distance/angle for HBOH in Model v are obviously more regular than HBNH, as shown in Fig. 14g and k. Apparently, the HBNH in Model v are also non-spontaneously formed as the HBOH in Model iv. In Model vi, H-bonds between DH−NH2 and CD are barely formed, so the number of HBNH and HBOH is quite limited (Fig. 14d) with the irregular frequency distribution of H-bonding distance/angle (Fig. 14h and l). It can be inferred that without the constraint of the force potential, hardly will be any type of H-bonds spontaneously formed between DH−NH2 and CDs.

Based on the above discussion about the H-bonding interaction in Model i, iv, v and vi, it can be concluded that the presence of protonated amines (i.e., −NH3 +) is necessary for the formation of HBNH, whereas the unprotonated amines (−NH2) seems incompetent at forming stable H-bonds. The premise of the formation of a larger number of HBOH is the deprotonation of amine group, which indicates that phenolic hydroxyl group (−OH) is at a disadvantage to compete with −NH3 + for H-bonding sites. Furthermore, only one deprotonated phenolic hydroxyl (−O−) will be enough to prevent the formation of HBOH.

For the anionic species of DPA (i.e., DH−NH2 and D2−NH2), their adsorption onto CDs is mediated by Na+ in the form of electrostatic attraction, which is a special adsorption mode differing from the H-bonding of other DPA species as mentioned above. Na+ are found to be able to achieve stable adsorption on the negatively charged surface of CDs, which provides abundant anchoring sites for −O−. The intermediation of the bridging Na+ explains the greater distance between the COMs of two types of anion DPA species and CDs after reaching the state of ΔE max (Fig. 13). In order to identify the difference in adsorption behavior between DH−NH2 and D2−NH2, the adsorption dynamics was monitored in the process of approaching the state of ΔE max, according to the trajectory of the corresponding sampling window. Fig. 15 shows the frequency distribution of the displacement of −NH2, −O−, −OH and Na+ relative to the geometric center of their trajectory in the most stable adsorption state of DH−NH2 and D2−NH2. The cloud maps representing the time-space density distribution of the functional groups are also provided in the insets, where the darker region stands for a higher probability of atoms appearing here.

From Fig. 15, it can be clearly seen that the displacement ranges of −O− and the bridging Na+ in Model vii are more concentrated than that in Model vi. However, the displacement distribution of −OH in DH−NH2 is more dispersed than that of −O−, indicating that −OH seems incapable of forming a stable and direct adsorption, but just hovering around the immediate vicinity of CDs surface, which merely benefits from the electrostatic attraction between −O− and Na+. In addition, −NH2 is even unable to get close to the surface of CDs, hovering over a larger space region. In general, the electrostatic interaction in the form of −COO− CDs-Na+-−O− surpasses the attraction of −COO− CDs to −NH2 or −OH in strength, and the chelated structure of the dual −O− mediated by Na+ is more robust than a single pair of Na+-−O− interaction. This well explains the phenomenon that ΔE max of D2−NH2 exceeds DH−NH2 and DH2NH2 (Fig. 13), since the adsorption of DPA anions by chelation of metal ions can be geometrically and mechanically superior. Overall, the effect of Na+ is found to act actively on the adsorption of anionic DPA species on CD, but limitedly on the non-anionic DPA species. For DH−NH2 and D2−NH2 species, the mediation of Na+ is the necessary condition for their adsorption, that is, the anionic species will search for the site of −COO− CDs-Na+ to achieve their adsorption on CDs.

The pH-dependent self-assembly of CDs-DPA NCs in solution environments were investigated by means of classical MD simulations in this work. The H-bonding interaction between the polar groups in DPA (e.g., amine and phenolic hydroxyl) and the acidic oxygenic groups (e.g., −COOHCDs) on the surface of CDs is the main driving force for the adsorption of DPA to CDs. Moreover, the protonation/deprotonation state of the −COOHCDs and the variation in DPA species with the change of pH conditions are responsible for the corresponding pH-dependent interaction behaviors between DPA and CDs.

Under acidic conditions, the adsorption affinity of CDs to DPA increases as pH rises, which is mainly attributed to the growing ratio of −COO− CDs that makes CDs more attractive to the single dominant DPA species (i.e., DH2NH3 +) in the system, but the aggregation of DPA is extremely limited. As the value of pH rises under alkaline conditions, the aggregation of DPA will increase first, but decrease then with the weakening adsorption of CDs to DPA. This is because the dominant DPA species in the system will go through a transition from cation to zwitterion, and then to anion as the pH increases, among which, only the cationic species shows high affinity for CDs, while the zwitterionic species tends to self-aggregate instead of being adsorbed on CDs, and the anion species was repelled by the negatively charged surface of CDs. Therefore, it can be inferred that the strongly acidic or alkaline environment is unfavorable to the self-assembly of CDs-DPA NCs, however, the weakly acidic or alkaline conditions are conducive to the self-assembly by different mechanisms. In weakly acidic solution with pH < 6.5, CDs can hold the greatest compaction of DPA coating, while in the weakly alkaline condition with pH ≤ 8.5, the adsorption capacity and strength of CDs to DPA is the highest, resulting from the most active H-bonding interaction. At pH∼10, DPA has a great aggregating performance, which is unfavorable for the activity of the interface interaction between DPA and CDs, but can enable DPA to adsorb on CDs in form of clusters. In addition, Na+ is found to act as bridge, capable of mediating the adsorption of DPA on CDs surface under strongly alkaline conditions by means of general electrostatic interaction. Analysis based on the PMF shows that Na+-mediated adsorption can even surpass the H-bonding of CDs-DPA in strength when the adsorbed DPA molecule is in the completely deprotonated anionic state (i.e., D2−NH2).

Our work devotes to guiding the synthesis of medically oriented NCs on the basis of CDs and PDA. We believe that the underlying interaction mechanism of CDs-DPA we revealed at the molecular scale may provide new insights into potential therapeutic strategies for the pH-triggered drug loading/release and biocompatible improvement of carbon-based NCs.
